pages.title: Trace formula report
pages.html:
2 Trace Form Report

In this text, I tried to expose Grothendieck's cohomological theory of functions as directly as possible \(L\). I am very close to some of the presentations given by Grothendieck to IHES in the spring of 1966. In the spirit of this volume, SGA 5 will not be called upon to make two references to passages from exposition XI, independent of the rest of this seminar.

In §10 (p.81-90) of [13], the reader will find a summary of the theory, in the crucial case of curves.

pages.title: Some reminders
pages.html:
1 Some reminders

1.1 Notes

\(p,q,\mathbb{F},\mathbb{F}_q\): \(p\) is a prime number, \(q=p^f\) a power of \(p\) and \(\mathbb{F}\) an algebraic fencing of the prime field \(\mathbb{F}_p\). For any power \(p^n\) of \(p\), we note \(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}\) the sub-field to \(p^n\) elements of \(\mathbb{F}\).

\(X_0,X\): Often a symbol affected by an index will be used \(0\) an object on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). The same symbol, without \(0\), means the object which is deduced from it by extension of scalar to \(\mathbb{F}\)For example, if \(\mathscr{F}_0\) is a sheaf on a diagram \(X_0\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), we note \(\mathscr{F}\) his reciprocal image on \(X=X_0\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q} \mathbb{F}\).

\(F\): If \(X_0\) is a scheme on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), we call morphism of Frobenius and we note \(F\) the endomorphism of \(X_0\) that "sends the coordinates \(x\) to the coordinates \(x^q\)": it is the identifier on the underlying topological space and, for \(x\) a local section of the structural sheaf, \(F^* x = x^q\).

The endomorphism of \(X\), which is deduced by scalar extension, is still referred to by \(F\). On the set \(X(\mathbb{F}) = X_0(\mathbb{F})\) of the rational points of \(X\), \(F\) acts as the substitution of Frobenius \(\varphi\in\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}/\mathbb{F}_q)\) defined by \(\varphi(x)=x^q\). In particular, \(X^F=X_0(\mathbb{F}_q)\).

In case of ambiguity, we will write \(F_{(q)}\) rather than \(F\).

1.2 Correspondance of Frobenius

Is \(X_0\) a diagram on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a sheaf on \(X_0\). I like to see as follows the correspondence of Frobenius (a \(F\)-endomorphism of \(\mathscr{F}_0\)):

\[ \tag{1.2.1} F^* : F^*\mathscr{F}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{F}_0 \]

We look at \(\mathscr{F}_0\) as the sheaf of the local sections of a space \([\mathscr{F}_0]\) spread over \(X_0\) (\([\mathscr{F}_0]\) is an algebraic space within the meaning of Mr. Artin). The function of \(F\) provides a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ [\mathscr{F}_0] \ar[r]^-F \ar[d] & [\mathscr{F}_0] \ar[d] \\ X_0 \ar[r]^F & X_0 } \]

Because \([\mathscr{F}_0]\) is spread over \(X_0\), this diagram is Cartesian; it provides isomorphism \([\mathscr{F}_0]\xrightarrow{\sim} F^*[\mathscr{F}_0]\) inversely (1.2.1) It is still noted. \(F^*\) correspondences or morphisms derived from \(F\) by extension of the scalar or by functionality, such as \(F^*:\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F})\).

For a definition in form, I refer to [21]. This is only the case where \(q=p\), but the general case is treated the same.

1.3

The correspondence of Frobenius is functional in \((X_0,\mathscr{F}_0)\). If \(f_0:X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_0\) is a morphism of \(\mathbb{F}_q\)-schemes, \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a sheaf on \(X_0\), \(\mathscr{G}_0\) a sheaf on \(Y_0\) and \(u\in\hom(f_0^*\mathscr{G}_0,\mathscr{F}_0)\) a \(f_0\)-morphism of \(\mathscr{G}_0\) in \(\mathscr{F}_0\), we have a commutative diagram of spaces \(f_0 F = F f_0\), and above a commutative diagram of sheaves

\[ \xymatrix{ X_0 \ar[r]^-{f_0} \ar[d]^-F & Y_0 \ar[d]^-F \\ X_0 \ar[r]^-{f_0} & Y_0 }\qquad\qquad \xymatrix{ \mathscr{F}_0 & \mathscr{G}_0 \ar[l]_-u\\ \mathscr{F}_0 \ar[u]_-{F^*} & \mathscr{G}_0 \ar[u]^-{F^*} \ar[l]} \]

In particular, the correspondence on \(f_{0*}\mathscr{F}_0\) deduced the correspondence of Frobenius from \((X_0,\mathscr{F}_0)\) is the correspondence of Frobenius from \((U_0,f_{0*}\mathscr{F}_0)\).

1.4

The natural morphism \(Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_0\) is a limit of staggering morphisms. As a result, for any abelian sheaf \(\mathscr{F}_0\) on \(X_0\), the reciprocal images on \(Y\) of \(\mathsf{R}^if_0\mathscr{F}_0\) are \(\mathsf{R}^if_*\mathscr{F}\).

On \(\mathsf{R}^if_*\mathscr{F}\), we have

  1. the correspondence \(F^*\mathsf{R}^i f_*\mathscr{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{R}^i f_*\mathscr{F} \xrightarrow{F^*} \mathsf{R}^if_*\mathscr{F}\) deduced by functionality of the Frobenius correspondence;

  2. the correspondence \(F^*\mathsf{R}^if_*\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{R}^if_*\mathscr{F}\) reciprocal image on \(Y\) of the correspondence of Frobenius on \(\mathsf{R}^ii f_{0*}\mathscr{F}_0\).

Deducted from (1.3) that these correspondences coincide. They induce the initial term of an action of Frobenius over the entire spectral sequence.

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^p(Y,\mathsf{R}^q f_*\mathscr{F}) \rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

In the same way in self-supporting cohomology, and for the spectral sequence

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^p(Y,\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}) \rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{F}) \]

1.5 Finished field change

Been \((X_0,\mathscr{F}_0)\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(n\) one integer, and \((X_1,\mathscr{F}_1)\) on \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\) deducted from \((X_0,\mathscr{F}_0)\) by extension of the scalar. One part of the correspondence of Frobenius is available \(F_{(q)}:X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0)\), \(F_{(q)}^*:F_{(q)}^*\mathscr{F}_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_0\), on the other hand, \(F_{(q^n)}:X_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_1\), \(F_{(q^n)}^*:F_{(q^n)}^*\mathscr{F}_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_1\). It is easy to check that \(F_{(q^n)}\) deduced from power \(n\)-th of \(F_{(q)}\) by extension of scalar \(\mathbb{F}_q\) to \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\); after extension of scalar to \(\mathbb{F}\), we still have the same identity \(F_{(q^n)}=F_{(q)}^n\) between endomorphisms of \(X\) and correspondence on \(X\).

If \(x\) is a closed point of \(X_0\), with \([k(x),\mathbb{F}_q]=n\), and \(\overline{x}\in X_0(\mathbb{F}) = X(\mathbb{F})\) is located in \(x\), \(\overline{x}\) is a fixed point of \(F_{(q^n)}=F_{(q)}^n\). Note \(F_x^*\) the endomorphism of \(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{x}}\) induced by \(F_{(q^n)}^*\). Isomorphism near, \((F_{\overline{x}}^*,\mathscr{F}_{\overline{x}})\) does not depend on the choice of \(\overline{x}\). The trace, etc. of \(F_x^*\) is referred to by a notation such as \(\operatorname{tr}(F_x^*,\mathscr{F})\).

Function \(L\)L

Either \(X_0\) a pattern of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\). We note \(|X_0|\) the set of its closed points and, for \(x\in |X_0|\), we place \(\deg(x)=[k(x),\mathbb{F}_p]\). If \(\mathscr{F}_0\) is a \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-sheaf on \(X_0\) (for this concept, see below) – or a constructible and flat sheaf of modules on a commutative noetherian ring \(A\), we note \(L(X_0,\mathscr{F}_0)\) the formal series of constant terms \(1\), \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell[\![ t]\!]\) or \(A[\![ t]\!]\),

\[ L(X_0,\mathscr{F}_0) = \prod_{x\in|X_0|} \det\left(1-F_x^* t^{\deg(x)},\mathscr{F}\right)^{-1} \text{.} \]

1.7 Note

In the definition (1.6), we have \(\deg(x)=[k(x),\mathbb{F}_p]\) (absolute degree) and not \(\deg(x)=[k(x),\mathbb{F}_q]\) (degree relative to \(\mathbb{F}_q\)). This has the effect that \(L(X_0,\mathscr{F}_0)\) depends only on the scheme \(X_0\) and the sheaf \(\mathscr{F}_0\), not on \(q\) and structural morphism \(X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F}_q)\). The unknown \(t\) is an avatar of \(p^{-s}\).

1.8 The Galoisian Perspective

This n\(^\circ\) will not be used in the rest of the report. For \(\mathscr{F}_0\) an abelian sheaf on \(X_0\), the group of Galois \(\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{F}/\mathbb{F}_q)\) acts on \(\mathbb{F}\), and by structural transport on \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\). In particular, the substitution of Frobenius \(\varphi\) (1.1) induces automorphism, again noted \(\varphi\), of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\). Frobenius' correspondence defines an endomorphism \(F^*\) of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\).

\[ \tag{1.8.1} {F^*}^{-1} = \varphi \qquad \text{(on } \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \text{).} \]

To see it, let's apply it (1.4) in case \(Y_0\) is a point: \(Y_0=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F}_q)\). It is found that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) is the fiber, at the geometric point \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F})\) of \(Y_0\), of \(\mathsf{R}^i f_! \mathscr{F}_0\), and that \(F^*\) is the fiber of the correspondence of Frobenius of \(\mathsf{R}^i f_!\mathscr{F}_0\). Either \([\mathsf{R}^i f_! \mathscr{F}_0]\) as in (1.2) We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = [\mathsf{R}^i f_!\mathscr{F}_0](\mathbb{F})\) and

  1. \(\varphi\) acts by structural transport, via its action on \(\mathbb{F}\),

  2. \(F^*\) is the opposite of \(F:[\mathsf{R}^i f_!\mathscr{F}_0](\mathbb{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} [\mathsf{R}^i f_!\mathscr{F}_0](\mathbb{F})\).

We conclude with the identity \(F=\varphi\) of (1.1).

One of the Galoisian interests is that it allows to reason by transport of structure.

pages.title: \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)Ql-sheaves
pages.html:
2 \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)Ql-sheaves

The concept of \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-sheaves is developed in detail in [21]. We will only use the definitions and results below.

Definition. Be \(X\) a noetherian scheme. A \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) is a projective sheaf system \(\mathscr{F}_n\) (\(n\geqslant 0\)), with \(\mathscr{F}_n\) a sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^{n+1}\)-constructible modules [2], and such as transition morphism \(\mathscr{F}_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_{n-1}\) is factored by an isomorph

\[ \mathscr{F}_n\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/\ell^{n+1}} \mathbb{Z}/\ell^n \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{F}_{n-1}\text{.} \]

It is said that \(\mathscr{F}\) is smooth if \(\mathscr{F}_n\) are locally constant. Definition.

The terminology of [21] is different, it says "\(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-raise constructible" for "\(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-raise," and "constant twistable constructible" for "raise."

Similarly, below, for \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-sheaves.

2.2

If \(k\) is a separably enclosed field, a sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-modules on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) is identified with a \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-module, and the \(\mathscr{F}\mapsto \varprojlim \mathscr{F}_n\) character identifies \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-sheaves on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) sux \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-modules of finite type. This justifies the following definition: fibre of \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) at the geometric point \(x\) of \(X\) is \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-module \(\varprojlim (\mathscr{F}_n)_x\).

2.3

Suppose \(X\) related, and be \(x\) a geometric dot of \(X\). It is known that the "fiber in \(x\)" is an equivalence of the locally constant constructible sheaf category of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-modules with the category of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-finite type modules with a continuous action of \(\pi_1(X,x)\). By going to the limit, the following proposition is derived.

Proposition. Under the above assumptions, the "fiber in \(x\)" is an equivalence of the category of \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-sheaves smooth on \(X\) with that of \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-modules of finite type equipped with a continuous action of \(\pi_1(X,x)\). Proposition.

Proposition. Be \(\mathscr{F}\) a \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-sheaf on a noetherian scheme \(X\). There is a finished partition of \(X\) in locally closed parts \(X_i\) such that \(\mathscr{F}|X_i\) are smooth. Proposition.

Possions \(\operatorname{gr}_\ell^n\mathscr{F}=\ell^n\mathscr{F}_m/\ell^{m+1}\mathscr{F}_m\) for \(m\geqslant n\), and \(\operatorname{gr}_\ell\mathscr{F}=\bigoplus \operatorname{gr}_\ell^n\mathscr{F}\). It is a bundle of modules on the noetherian ring \(\operatorname{gr}_\ell\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{Z}/\ell[t]\), graduated from \(\mathbb{Z}\) for filtration \(\ell\)-adic. It is a quotient of \(\operatorname{gr}_\ell^0\mathscr{F} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/\ell} \mathbb{Z}/\ell[t]\), therefore a sheaf of \(\operatorname{gr}_\ell\mathbb{Z}\)-constructible modules [2]. and there is a finished partition of \(X\) in locally closed parts \(X_i\), such as \(\operatorname{gr}_\ell \mathscr{F}|X_i\) are locally constant. Each of the sheaves \(\operatorname{gr}_\ell^n\mathscr{F}|X_i\) is then locally constant as well as the \(\mathscr{F}_m|X_i\), which are successive extensions.

2.6

If \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) are two \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-sheaves on \(X\), we place \(\hom(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) = \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}_n,\mathscr{G}_n)\); it is a \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-module. Since \(\hom(\mathscr{F}_n,\mathscr{G}_n) = \hom(\mathscr{F}_m,\mathscr{G}_n)\) for \(m\geqslant n\), we still have

\[ \hom(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) = \varprojlim_n \varinjlim_m \hom(\mathscr{F}_m,\mathscr{G}_n) \text{,} \]

pages.html:
3 Form of traces and functions \(L\)L

We use the ratings of §1, and \(\ell\) is a prime number \(\ne p\).

Been \(X_0\) a separate pattern of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) (\(q=p^f\)) and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) one \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-constructible sheaf on \(X_0\). Grothendieck's cohomological interpretation of functions \(L\) is the following theorem:

Theorem. \(L(X_0,\mathscr{F}_0) = \prod_i \det\left(1-F^* t^f, \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\right)^{(-1)^{i+1}}\). Theorem.

It is obtained as a consequence of the formula of traces:

Theorem. For the whole \(n\), \(\sum_{x\in X^{F^n}} \operatorname{tr}\left({F^n}^*,\mathscr{F}_x\right) = \sum_i (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}\left({F^n}^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\right)\). Theorem.

Let us briefly recall the classical method of deducing (3.1) of (3.2. Let's pose \(T=t^f\); both members are formal series in \(T\), of constant term \(1\). Since \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\) is characteristic \(0\), it is sufficient to prove that the logarithmic derivatives \(T\frac{d}{dT}\log\) of both members are equal.

To calculate these logarithmic derivatives, we will use the formula

\[ T \frac{d}{dT}\log\det(1-f T)^{-1} = \sum_{n>0} \operatorname{tr}(f^n) T^n \text{,} \]

the demonstration (in a somewhat more general context) is recalled below.

\begin{align*} T\frac{d}{dT}\log L(X_0,\mathscr{F}_0) &= \sum_{x\in |X_0|} \sum_n [k(x):\mathbb{F}_q] \operatorname{tr}\left(F_x^n,\mathscr{F}\right) T^{n\cdot[k(x):\mathbb{F}_q]} \\ &= \sum_{n>0} T^n \sum_{x\in X^{F^n}} \operatorname{tr}\left({F^n}^*,\mathscr{F}_x\right) \end{align*}

and second member

\[ \sum_{n>0} T^n \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}\left({F^n}^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\right) \text{ ; } \]

We're comparing term to term.

Proposition. We have \(A\) a commutative ring, \(M\) a \(A\)-projective module of finite type and \(f\) an endomorphism of \(M\). For any inversable formal series \(Q\), we have \(\frac{d}{dT}\log Q = Q^{-1}\frac{d}{dT}Q\).

\[ \tag{3.3.1} T\frac{d}{dT}\log\det(1-f T)^{-1} = \sum_{n>0} \operatorname{tr}(f^n) T^n \text{.} \]

Proposition.

Either \(M'\) a \(A\)-module such that \(M\oplus M'\) is free of finite rank. Replace \((M,f)\) by \((M\oplus M',f\oplus 0)\) does not change the two members of (3.3), and brings us back to the case where \(M\) is free. For \(M=A^d\), (3.3.1) is an algebraic identity relating to the coefficients \(f_i^j\) of \(f\). By the principle of prolongation of algebraic identities, it is sufficient to verify it for \(A\) an algebraically closed field of characteristic \(0\). Both members being additives in \(M\) in an extension \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\), it is even sufficient to treat only the case where \(M\) is of rank \(1\). If \(f\) is the multiplication by \(a\), (3.3.1) is then reduced to the formula

\[ T\frac{d}{dT}\log\left((1-a T)^{-1}\right) = \sum_{n>0} a^n T^n \text{.} \]

corollary_ Be \(n\) one integer, and \(f^{(n)}\) the endomorphism \((x_1,\dotsc,x_n)\mapsto \left(f(x_n),x_1,\dotsc,x_{n-1}\right)\) of \(M^n\).

\[ \tag{3.4.1} \det(1-f T^n) = \det(1-f^{(n)} T) \text{.} \]

corollary_

Process as in (3.3), it is assumed that \(A\) is characteristic \(0\). It is sufficient to verify that the logarithmic derivatives \(-T\frac{d}{dT}\log\) of both members are equal.

\begin{align*} -T\frac{d}{dT}\log\det(1-f T^n) &= - n T^n \frac{d}{dT^n} \log\det(1-f T^n) = n \sum_{m>0} \operatorname{tr}(f^m) T^{n m} \text{,} \\ -T \frac{d}{dT}\log\det\left(1-f^{(n)} T\right) &= \sum_{m>0} \operatorname{tr}\left(f^{(n) m}\right) T^m \text{,} \end{align*}

and it is observed that

\begin{align*} \operatorname{tr}(f^{(n) m}) &= 0 \qquad \text{if } n\nmid m \\ \operatorname{tr}(f^{(n) n m}) &= n \operatorname{tr}(f^m) \text{.} \end{align*}

3.5 Note

For \(A=\mathbb{Q}_\ell\), (3.4.1) is the special case \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\) of (3.1) Using this identity, it is easy to verify directly that the second member of (3.1) is independent of \(q\), and structural morphism \(X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F}_q)\).

3.6. Note

If \(j:X_0\hookrightarrow \overline{X}_0\) is a compaction of \(X_0\), we have \(L(X_0,\mathscr{F}_0) = L(\overline{X}_0, j_!\mathscr{F}_0)\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_!\mathscr{F})\). This brings back (3.1) to the proper case, and explains the use of the compact support cohomology. For \(X\) clean, (3.2) has the shape of a formula of the traces of Lefschetz. It should be noted that the local terms \(\operatorname{tr}(F^{n *},\mathscr{F}_x)\) (\(x\in X^{F^n}\)) depend only on the fiber of \(\mathscr{F}\) in \(x\), and are not affected by multiplicity. For \(X\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) smooth, this corresponds to the fact that the graph of \(F\) is transverse to the diagonal (since \(d F = 0\)).

3.7. Note

If we admit the formalism of \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-sheaves (cf. 2.11; the Leray spectral sequence and the basic change theorem for higher direct images must be available \(\mathsf{R}^q f_!\)), it is easy to bring back the proof of (3.1, 3.2), in case \(X_0\) is a smooth curve and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) is smooth. This is clearly explained in [22] (for 3.1; 3.2 is treated as well).

3.8

There are two methods of proving (3.2).

Lefschetz-Verdier

If \(X_0\) is clean (cf. 3.6), the formula of the general traces of Lefschetz-Verdier makes it possible to express the second member of (3.2) as a sum of local terms, one for each point of \(X^{F^n}\). In the original version of [21], this formula was proved only modulo the resolution of singularities. The reader will find unconditional proof in the final version. In the case of curves, case to which one can reduce (3.7), the ingredients of the demonstration were all available.

To deduct (3.2) of the formula of Lefschetz-Verdier, one must be able to calculate the local terms. For a curve and the endomorphism of Frobenius, this had been done by Artin and Verdier [40].

Nielson-Wecken

A method inspired by the works of Nielsen and Wecken allows to bring back (3.2), to a particular case proved by Weil; that is what will be explained in the following paragraphs.

Proposition. We have \(A\) a commutative ring, \(M\) a \(A\)-projective module of finite type and \(f\) an endomorphism of \(M\). For any inversable formal series \(Q\), we have \(\frac{d}{dT}\log Q = Q^{-1}\frac{d}{dT}Q\).
\[ \tag{3.3.1} T\frac{d}{dT}\log\det(1-f T)^{-1} = \sum_{n>0} \operatorname{tr}(f^n) T^n \text{.} \]
Proposition.

Either \(M'\) a \(A\)-module such that \(M\oplus M'\) is free of finite rank. Replace \((M,f)\) by \((M\oplus M',f\oplus 0)\) does not change the two members of (3.3), and brings us back to the case where \(M\) is free. For \(M=A^d\), (3.3.1) is an algebraic identity relating to the coefficients \(f_i^j\) of \(f\). By the principle of prolongation of algebraic identities, it is sufficient to verify it for \(A\) an algebraically closed field of characteristic \(0\). Both members being additives in \(M\) in an extension \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\), it is even sufficient to treat only the case where \(M\) is of rank \(1\). If \(f\) is the multiplication by \(a\), (3.3.1) is then reduced to the formula
\[ T\frac{d}{dT}\log\left((1-a T)^{-1}\right) = \sum_{n>0} a^n T^n \text{.} \]
corollary_ Be \(n\) one integer, and \(f^{(n)}\) the endomorphism \((x_1,\dotsc,x_n)\mapsto \left(f(x_n),x_1,\dotsc,x_{n-1}\right)\) of \(M^n\).
\[ \tag{3.4.1} \det(1-f T^n) = \det(1-f^{(n)} T) \text{.} \]
corollary_
Process as in (3.3), it is assumed that \(A\) is characteristic \(0\). It is sufficient to verify that the logarithmic derivatives \(-T\frac{d}{dT}\log\) of both members are equal.

\begin{align*} -T\frac{d}{dT}\log\det(1-f T^n) &= - n T^n \frac{d}{dT^n} \log\det(1-f T^n) = n \sum_{m>0} \operatorname{tr}(f^m) T^{n m} \text{,} \\ -T \frac{d}{dT}\log\det\left(1-f^{(n)} T\right) &= \sum_{m>0} \operatorname{tr}\left(f^{(n) m}\right) T^m \text{,} \end{align*}
and it is observed that
\begin{align*} \operatorname{tr}(f^{(n) m}) &= 0 \qquad \text{if } n\nmid m \\ \operatorname{tr}(f^{(n) n m}) &= n \operatorname{tr}(f^m) \text{.} \end{align*}

For \(A=\mathbb{Q}_\ell\), (3.4.1) is the special case \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F}_{q^n})\) of (3.1) Using this identity, it is easy to verify directly that the second member of (3.1) is independent of \(q\), and structural morphism \(X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F}_q)\).

If \(j:X_0\hookrightarrow \overline{X}_0\) is a compaction of \(X_0\), we have \(L(X_0,\mathscr{F}_0) = L(\overline{X}_0, j_!\mathscr{F}_0)\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},j_!\mathscr{F})\). This brings back (3.1) to the proper case, and explains the use of the compact support cohomology. For \(X\) clean, (3.2) has the shape of a formula of the traces of Lefschetz. It should be noted that the local terms \(\operatorname{tr}(F^{n *},\mathscr{F}_x)\) (\(x\in X^{F^n}\)) depend only on the fiber of \(\mathscr{F}\) in \(x\), and are not affected by multiplicity. For \(X\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) smooth, this corresponds to the fact that the graph of \(F\) is transverse to the diagonal (since \(d F = 0\)).

If we admit the formalism of \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-sheaves (cf. 2.11; the Leray spectral sequence and the basic change theorem for higher direct images must be available \(\mathsf{R}^q f_!\)), it is easy to bring back the proof of (3.1, 3.2), in case \(X_0\) is a smooth curve and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) is smooth. This is clearly explained in [22] (for 3.1; 3.2 is treated as well).
There are two methods of proving (3.2).
Lefschetz-Verdier

If \(X_0\) is clean (cf. 3.6), the formula of the general traces of Lefschetz-Verdier makes it possible to express the second member of (3.2) as a sum of local terms, one for each point of \(X^{F^n}\). In the original version of [21], this formula was proved only modulo the resolution of singularities. The reader will find unconditional proof in the final version. In the case of curves, case to which one can reduce (3.7), the ingredients of the demonstration were all available.
To deduct (3.2) of the formula of Lefschetz-Verdier, one must be able to calculate the local terms. For a curve and the endomorphism of Frobenius, this had been done by Artin and Verdier [40].
Nielson-Wecken
A method inspired by the works of Nielsen and Wecken allows to bring back (3.2), to a particular case proved by Weil; that is what will be explained in the following paragraphs.

4 Reduction to theorems in \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)Z/l n-cohomology

We will prove (3.2) by passing to the limit from a similar theorem in \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-cohomology. The statement presents the following difficulty. Is \(X_0\) a separate scheme of the finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a flat and constructible sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-modules. Its fibres are \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-free modules of the finite type, and the left member of (3.2) therefore has a meaning (it is an element of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)). On the other hand, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) will not in general be free, so the right-wing member is not defined. Obvier to this difficulty is one of the essential uses made by Grothendieck of the theory of derived categories.

4.1

For the definition of the derived category \(\mathsf{D}(\mathscr{A})\) of an abelian category \(\mathscr{A}\), and that of the sub-category \(\mathsf{D}^+(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{D}^-(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{D}^b(\mathscr{A})\), I refer to the text of Verdier in this volume. For \(\Lambda\) a ring is written \(\mathsf{D}(\Lambda)\) instead of \(\mathsf{D}(\text{category } \Lambda \text{-modules on the left})\); for \(X\) a site is written \(\mathsf{D}(X,\Lambda)\) instead of \(\mathsf{D}(\) category of sheaves of \(\Lambda\)-modules left\()\). Similarly for \(\mathsf{D}^-\), etc...

The sign \(\mathsf{R}\) (rep. \(\mathsf{L}\)) indicates a right derivative (left resp.). For example, \(\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\) indicates the sensor derived from the tensor product: for \(K\) in \(\mathsf{D}^-(\Lambda^\circ)\) and \(L\) in \(\mathsf{D}^-(\Lambda)\) (\(\Lambda^\circ\) ring opposite to \(\Lambda\)), \(K\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} L\) is calculated as follows: quasi-isomorphisms are taken \(K'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\), \(L'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\), with \(K'\) and \(L\) upper bounded and one of them with flat components, and the simple complex \(s(K'\otimes_\Lambda L)\) associated with the double complex \(K'\otimes_\Lambda L'\).

4.2

Even to deal with the only case of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-cohomology, we will need the theory of the traces of endomorphisms of modules on not necessarily commutative rings (algebras of finite groups \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n[G]\)). These traces were introduced by Stallings and Hattori [3].

Either \(\Lambda\) a ring (unitary, but not necessarily commutative). \(\Lambda^\natural\) the quotient of the additive group of \(\Lambda\) by the sub-group generated by the switches \(a b - b a\). If \(f\) is an endomorphism of the \(\Lambda\)-free left module \(\Lambda^n\), of matrix \(f_i^j\), we note \(\operatorname{tr}(f)\) the image of \(\sum f_i^f\) in \(\Lambda^\natural\). If \(f\) and \(g\) are two homomorphisms \(f:\Lambda^n \leftrightarrows \Lambda^m : g\), we check as soon as \(\operatorname{tr}(f g) = \operatorname{tr}(g f)\).

Either \(f\) an endomorphism of a \(\Lambda\)-finished projective module \(P\). Let's write \(P\) as a direct factor of a free module \(\Lambda^n\): this means choosing a module \(P'\), and an isomorphism \(\alpha:P\oplus P'\xrightarrow{\sim} \Lambda^n\), either still choosing a homomorphism \(a:P\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Lambda^n\), and a retraction \(b:\Lambda^n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P\) (take \(P=\ker(b)\)). Or \(f'=\alpha(f\oplus 0)\alpha^{-1} =af b\). We pose \(\operatorname{tr}(f) = \operatorname{tr}(f')\). This element of \(\Lambda^\natural\) depends only on \(f\): if another choice \(c:P\leftrightarrows \Lambda^m:d\) is made, we have \(a=a(dc)(ba)\), from where

\[ \operatorname{tr}(a f b) = \operatorname{tr}(adcbafb) = \operatorname{tr}(cbafbad) = \operatorname{tr}(cfd) \text{.} \]

If \(f\) is an endomorphism of a \(\Lambda\)-finished projective module \(\mathbb{Z}/2\)-graded, of components \(f_i^j:P^j\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P^i\), are installed

\[ \operatorname{tr}(f) = \operatorname{tr}(f_0^0) - \operatorname{tr}(f_1^1) \text{.} \]

If there is a risk of ambiguity, we will write instead \(\operatorname{tr}(f;P)\). If the homomorphisms \(f:P\leftrightarrows Q:g\) are homogeneous, we immediately check by reduction to the free case that

\[ \tag{4.2.1} \operatorname{tr}(f g) = \operatorname{tr}\left((-1)^{\deg f \deg g} g f\right) \text{.} \]

If \(f\) is an endomorphism of a \(\Lambda\)-module \(\mathbb{Z}/2\)-graded filtered \((P,F)\), finished filtration, compatible with graduation, and \(\operatorname{gr}_F^i(P)\) are projective finite type, then \(P\) is projective finite type, and

\[ \tag{4.2.2} \operatorname{tr}(f;P) = \sum \operatorname{tr}\left(f; \operatorname{gr}_F^i(P) \right) \]

(crack the filtration to return to the case of a direct sum).

4.3

A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-graded module will be considered \(\mathbb{Z}/2\)-graded by degree parity. In particular, if \(f\) is an endomorphism of a limited complex of \(\Lambda\)-finished projective modules, one poses

\[ \tag{4.3.1} \operatorname{tr}(f) = \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}(f^i) \text{.} \]

According to (4.2.1), if \(f\) is homotope at zero, \(\operatorname{tr}(f) = 0\):

\[ \tag{4.3.2} f = d H + h D \rightarrow \operatorname{tr}(f) = 0 \text{.} \]

Either \(\mathsf{K}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\) the category of objects the complexes bounded of \(\Lambda\)-projective modules of the finite type, and arrows the morphisms of complexes taken to homotopy. The figurator \(\mathsf{K}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{D}(\Lambda)\) is fully faithful. We note \(\mathsf{D}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\) his essential image. The formulas (4.3.1) (4.3.2) allow to define \(\operatorname{tr}(f)\) for \(f\) an endomorphism of \(K\in\operatorname{Ob} \mathsf{D}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\) (and this trace depends only on the isomorph class of \((K,f)\)).

4.4

Note that the filtered derivative category \(\mathsf{DF}(\mathscr{A})\) of an abelian category \(\mathscr{A}\) is the category derived from that of the filtered finite filtration object complexes of \(\mathscr{A}\) by reversing filtered quasi-isomorphisms (\(=\) morphisms of filtered complexes that induce quasi-isomorphism on the associated graduated). There are "underlying complexes": \(\mathsf{DF}(\mathscr{A})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{D}(\mathscr{A}): (K,F)\mapsto K\) and "\(p\)Eighth graduated component": \(\mathsf{DF}(\mathscr{A})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{D}(\mathscr{A}): (K,F)\mapsto \operatorname{gr}_F^p(K)\).

Either \(\mathsf{KF}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\) the category of objects the filtered finite filtration complexes \((K,F)\) with \(\operatorname{gr}_F^p(K^q)\) projectives of finite type, and arrows the morphisms of complexes respecting filtration, caught at a homotopia respecting filtration. The \(\mathsf{KF}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{DF}(\Lambda)\) is fully faithful; its essential image \(\mathsf{DF}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\) is almost all null; if \((K,F)\) is in \(\mathsf{D}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\), then \(K\) is in \(\mathsf{D}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\).

According to (4.2.2), if \(f\) is an endomorphism of \((K,F)\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{DF}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\), we have

\[ \tag{4.4.1} \operatorname{tr}(f,K) = \sum_p \operatorname{tr}(f,\operatorname{gr}_F^p(K)) \text{.} \]

4.5

Be \(\Lambda\) a ring, and \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^-(\Lambda)\). It is said that \(K\) is of tor-dimension \(\leqslant r\) if for all \(\Lambda\)-module to the right \(N\), the hypertors \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}_\Lambda K)\) are null for \(i<-r\). It is said of finished tor-dimension if this condition is verified for a \(r\).

The same terminology is used for a sheaf complex \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^-(X,\Lambda)\).

Lemma 4.5.1

Be \(\Lambda\) a Noetherian ring on the left, and \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^-(\Lambda)\). To be \(K\) in \(\mathsf{DF}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\), it is necessary and sufficient that it is of finished tor-dimensional and that the \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(K)\) be of finite type.

If you want to replace \(K\) by an isomorphic complex (in \(\mathsf{D}^-(\Lambda)\)), you can assume \(K\) bounded above and with free components of the finite type (cf. 4.7, below). If \(K\) is of tor-dimension \(\leqslant r\), \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(K)\) is invalid for \(i<-r\), \(K^{-r}/\operatorname{im}(d)\) admits free resolution

\[ (\cdots \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{-r-1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{-r}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{-r}/\operatorname{im}(d) \text{,} \]

and for everything \(\Lambda\)-module right \(N\), \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{-r-k}(N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}_\Lambda K) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{-r-k}(N\otimes_\Lambda K) = \operatorname{Tor}_k(N,K^r/\operatorname{im} d)\) for \(k\geqslant 1\).

By hypothesis, these groups are null; the module \(K^r/\operatorname{im} d\) is thus flat of finite presentation, i.e. projective of finite type. The quotient

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^r/\operatorname{im}(d) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{r+1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{r+2}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \]

of \(K\) is almost isomorphic to \(K\), and this shows that \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{DF}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\).

Proposition-Definition. The following equivalent conditions shall be verified: \(X\) a Noetherian scheme, and \(\Lambda\) a Noetherian ring on the left. \(\mathsf{D}_\textnormal{ctf}^b(X,\Lambda)\) the subcategory of \(\mathsf{D}^-(X,\Lambda)\) consisting of \(\mathscr{K}\) complexes:

  1. \(\mathscr{K}\) is isomorphic (in \(\mathsf{D}^-(X,\Lambda)\)) to a bounded complex of \(\Lambda\)-flat and constructible modules.

  2. \(\mathscr{K}\) is of finished tor-dimensional and the sheaves \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(K)\) are constructible.

Proposition-Definition.

The same argument as in (4.5), from the next lemma.

Lemma. If a \(\mathscr{K}\) sheaf complex of \(\Lambda\)-modules is such that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\mathscr{K}))\) are constructible, and void for \(i\) large, there is a quasi-isomorphism \(\mathscr{K}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{K}\), with \(\mathscr{K}'\) bounded superior to constructible and flat components. Lemma.

We build \(\mathscr{K}'\) by spreading from right to left. For \(m\) such that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\mathscr{K})\) (\(i\geqslant m\)) are null, we take \({\mathscr{K}'}^i = 0\) for \(i\geqslant m\). Suppose then already built \({\mathscr{K}'}^i\) for \(i>n\):

\[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & \mathscr{K}^{n-1} \ar[r] & \mathscr{K}^n \ar[r] & \mathscr{K}^{n+1} \ar[r] & \cdots \\ & & & \mathscr{K}'^{n+1} \ar[u] \ar[r] & \cdots , } \]

with \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\mathscr{K}')\xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\mathscr{K})\) for \(i>n+1\), and \(\ker(d)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{n+1}(\mathscr{K})\) surjective in degree \(n+1\). Set sheaves \(\mathscr{A}\) and \(\mathscr{B}\) by Cartesian diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{K}^n \ar[r] & \mathscr{K}^n/\operatorname{im}(d) \ar[r] & \ker(d) \\ \mathscr{A} \ar[u] \ar[r]^u & \mathscr{B} \ar[u]\ar[r] & \ker(d) \ar[u] . } \]

So, \(\mathscr{B}\) is constructible, \(u\) is overjective, and to move forward one step, just build \(K^n\) flat and constructible and \(v:\mathscr{K}^n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}\) such that u \(u v\) is overjective. That this is possible is guaranteed by the next gem.

Lemma. Either \(v:\mathscr{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{B}\) a sheaf epimorphism of \(\Lambda\)-modules, with \(\mathscr{B}\) constructible. There then exists \(u:\mathscr{K}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{A}\) with \(\mathscr{K}\) flat and constructible and \(v u\) an epimorphism. Lemma.

The sheaf \(\mathscr{A}\) is quotient of a sum of bundles of the form \(\varphi_! \Lambda\), for \(\varphi:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) span. Since \(\mathscr{B}\) is noetherian ([2]), a finite sum of these is already sent to \(\mathscr{B}\), hence the gem.

Theorem. Either \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a separated morphism of finite type of noetherian schemes. If \(\mathscr{K}\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\textnormal{ctf}^b(X,\Lambda)\), then \(\mathsf{R} f_!\mathscr{K}\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\textnormal{ctf}^b(Y,\Lambda)\). Theorem.

Note that if \(j:X\hookrightarrow \overline{X} \xrightarrow{\overline{f}} Y\) is a relative compaction of \(X/Y\), we have by definition \(\mathsf{R} f_! = \mathsf{R} \overline{f}_* \circ j_!\), where \(\mathsf{R} \overline{f}_*\) is the searcher derived from \(\overline{f}_*\). This formula brings back the proof of (4.9) in case \(f\) is clean. For \(f\) own, \(f_*\) is of finished cohomological dimension; this allows to define \(\mathsf{R} f_*\) on the entire derived category, and also as a searcher

\[ \mathsf{R} f_* : \mathsf{D}^-(X,\Lambda) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{D}^-(Y,\Lambda) \text{.} \]

By composition, the same is generally defined

\[ \mathsf{R} f_! : \mathsf{D}^-(X,\Lambda) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{D}^-(Y,\Lambda) \text{.} \]

For all \(\Lambda\)-module right \(N\), we have (proof given below)

\[ \tag{4.9.1} N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R} f_! \mathscr{K} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{R} f_! (N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathscr{K}) \text{.} \]

Deductions (4.9) of (4.9.1).

  1. The spectral sequence

    \[ E_2^{p q} = \mathsf{R}^p f_! \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\mathscr{K}) \rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{p+q} \mathsf{R} f_! \mathscr{K} \text{.} \]

    and the enditude theorem for \(\mathsf{R} f_!\), show that \(\mathsf{R} f_!\mathscr{K}\in \operatorname{Ob} \mathsf{D}^b(X,\Lambda)\) and is to constructible cohomology. It remains to prove the finished tor-dimensional (4.6).

  2. If \(\mathscr{K}\) is of tor-dimension \(\leqslant -r\), the same applies to \(\mathsf{R} f_! \mathscr{K}\): we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R} f_! \mathscr{K}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\mathsf{R} f_!(N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathscr{K}))\), and in the spectral sequence above for \(N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathscr{K}\), \(E_2^{p q}\) are void for \(q<r-s\) fortiori for \(p+q<r\).

Prove (4.9.1), remaining at the level of the complexes.

  1. Using the definition formula \(\mathsf{R} f_! = \mathsf{R} f_*\circ j_!\), one boils down to assuming \(f\) clean.

  2. Let's represent \(\mathscr{K}\) by a complex bounded superior to acyclic components for \(f_*:\mathsf{R}^p f_* \mathscr{K}^q = 0\) for \(p>0\). We then have \(\mathsf{R} f_* K \sim f_* \mathscr{K}\). It is only possible to work this way with complexes bounded superiorly because \(f_*\) is of finite cohomological dimension.

  3. If \(L\) a \(\Lambda\)-module to the right free, we have \(\mathsf{R}^p f_*(L\otimes \mathscr{K}^q) = L\otimes \mathsf{R}^p f_* \mathscr{K}^q\): it's trivial for \(\Lambda\) free finite type, and the general case is deduced by passing to the limit. The sheaf \(L\otimes \mathscr{K}^q\) is acyclic for \(f_*\), and \(f_*(L\otimes \mathscr{K}^q) = L\otimes f_* \mathscr{K}^q\).

  4. Either \(N_\bullet\) a free resolution of \(N\). We have \(N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathscr{K}\sim s(N_\bullet\otimes \mathscr{K})\) (simple complex associated with the double complex \(N_\bullet\otimes \mathscr{K}\)).

    \[ \mathsf{R} f_*(N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathscr{K}) \sim \mathsf{R} f_* (s(N_\bullet\otimes \mathscr{K})) \sim f_*(s(N_\bullet\otimes \mathscr{K})) \sim s(N_\bullet\otimes f_* \mathscr{K}) \sim N\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R} f_* \mathscr{K} \text{,} \]

    or the announced formula.

When \(Y\) is the spectrum of a separably closed field, the \(\mathsf{R} f_!\) will identify itself with a rated \(\mathsf{R} \Gamma_c : \mathsf{D}_\text{ctf}^b(X,\Lambda)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{D}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\).

Theorem. With the ratings of §1, be \(X_0\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(\Lambda\) a noetherian ring of torsion, killed by a first integer to \(p\). Either \(\mathscr{K}_0\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\textnormal{ctf}^b(X_0,\Lambda)\). With the definition (4.3) traces, we have for everything \(N\)

\[ \sum_{x\in X^{F^n}} \operatorname{tr}\left({F^n}^*,\mathscr{K}_x\right) = \operatorname{tr}\left({F^n}^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K})\right) \text{.} \]

Theorem.

In the end of this §, we will deduce 3.2 of the 4.10, and prove 2.10.

4.11 Evidence of 2.102.10

Either \(X\) a separate pattern of finite type on an algebraically closed field \(k\), and either \(\mathscr{G}\) a \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-sheaf on \(X\). Based on 2.8 it can be written in the form \(\mathscr{G}=\mathscr{F}\otimes\mathbb{Q}_\ell\), with \(\mathscr{F}\) a \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-sheaf without twist.

\[ K_n = \mathsf{R} \Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{F}\otimes\mathbb{Z}/\ell^{n+1})\in\mathsf{D}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^{n+1}) \text{.} \]

Based on 4.9, we have \(K_n\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\text{parf}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^{n+1})\), and it results from (4.9.1) only the natural arrow

\[ \tag{4.11.1} \xymatrix{ K_n\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathbb{Z}/\ell^{n+1}} \mathbb{Z}/\ell^n \ar[r] & K_{n+1} , } \]

is isomorphism. The problem of defining this arrow is discussed in 4.12.

We can now invoke [21] (p. 31, 1-2 at the end; this passage is independent of the rest of the [21]) to realize each \(K_n\) by a limited complex of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^{n+1}\)-free modules of finite type, and arrows (4.11.1) by isomorphisms of complexes

\[ \xymatrix{ K_n\otimes \mathbb{Z}/\ell^n \ar[r]^-\sim & K_{n-1} . } \]

Either \(K\) the complex \(\varprojlim K_n\). It is a limited complex of \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-free modules, and \(K_n\simeq K\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}_\ell}\mathbb{Z}/\ell^{n+1}\).

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(K) = \varprojlim \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(K_n) \text{,} \]

(any projective system of finished groups verifies the condition of Mittag-Leffler), and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(K)\) is a \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)-module of finite type.

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{G}) = \left(\varprojlim \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(K_n) \right)\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}_\ell} \mathbb{Q}_\ell \text{:} \]

it is a \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell\)-vectorial space of finite dimension.

The arrow (4.11.1)(4.11.1)

For define the arrow (4.11.1), it is not possible to proceed as in the case of 4.9, i.e. solve \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\) by a free \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^{n+1}\)-modules complex, since one wants to build on isomorphism in \(\mathsf{D}_\text{parf}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)\). The desired arrow is a particular case of the next scalar extension arrow.

(*) Either \(\Lambda\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Lambda'\) a homomorphism of torsion Noetherian rings. For \(X\) a separate pattern of finite type on \(k\) algebraically closed, and \(\mathscr{K}\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\text{ctf}(X,\Lambda)\), one has an isomorphism

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}_\Lambda \Lambda' \ar[r]^-\sim & \mathsf{R} \Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}_\Lambda \Lambda') . } \]

More general arrows are built in [2]The method is as follows:

blocks.html: 4 Reduction to theorems in \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)Z/l n-cohomology

We will prove (3.2) by passing to the limit from a similar theorem in \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-cohomology. The statement presents the following difficulty. Is \(X_0\) a separate scheme of the finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(\mathscr{F}_0\) a flat and constructible sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-modules. Its fibres are \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-free modules of the finite type, and the left member of (3.2) therefore has a meaning (it is an element of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)). On the other hand, \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,\mathscr{F})\) will not in general be free, so the right-wing member is not defined. Obvier to this difficulty is one of the essential uses made by Grothendieck of the theory of derived categories.

For the definition of the derived category \(\mathsf{D}(\mathscr{A})\) of an abelian category \(\mathscr{A}\), and that of the sub-category \(\mathsf{D}^+(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{D}^-(\mathscr{A})\), \(\mathsf{D}^b(\mathscr{A})\), I refer to the text of Verdier in this volume. For \(\Lambda\) a ring is written \(\mathsf{D}(\Lambda)\) instead of \(\mathsf{D}(\text{category } \Lambda \text{-modules on the left})\); for \(X\) a site is written \(\mathsf{D}(X,\Lambda)\) instead of \(\mathsf{D}(\) category of sheaves of \(\Lambda\)-modules left\()\). Similarly for \(\mathsf{D}^-\), etc...

The sign \(\mathsf{R}\) (rep. \(\mathsf{L}\)) indicates a right derivative (left resp.). For example, \(\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\) indicates the sensor derived from the tensor product: for \(K\) in \(\mathsf{D}^-(\Lambda^\circ)\) and \(L\) in \(\mathsf{D}^-(\Lambda)\) (\(\Lambda^\circ\) ring opposite to \(\Lambda\)), \(K\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} L\) is calculated as follows: quasi-isomorphisms are taken \(K'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\), \(L'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\), with \(K'\) and \(L\) upper bounded and one of them with flat components, and the simple complex \(s(K'\otimes_\Lambda L)\) associated with the double complex \(K'\otimes_\Lambda L'\).

Even to deal with the only case of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\)-cohomology, we will need the theory of the traces of endomorphisms of modules on not necessarily commutative rings (algebras of finite groups \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n[G]\)). These traces were introduced by Stallings and Hattori [3].

Either \(\Lambda\) a ring (unitary, but not necessarily commutative). \(\Lambda^\natural\) the quotient of the additive group of \(\Lambda\) by the sub-group generated by the switches \(a b - b a\). If \(f\) is an endomorphism of the \(\Lambda\)-free left module \(\Lambda^n\), of matrix \(f_i^j\), we note \(\operatorname{tr}(f)\) the image of \(\sum f_i^f\) in \(\Lambda^\natural\). If \(f\) and \(g\) are two homomorphisms \(f:\Lambda^n \leftrightarrows \Lambda^m : g\), we check as soon as \(\operatorname{tr}(f g) = \operatorname{tr}(g f)\).

Either \(f\) an endomorphism of a \(\Lambda\)-finished projective module \(P\). Let's write \(P\) as a direct factor of a free module \(\Lambda^n\): this means choosing a module \(P'\), and an isomorphism \(\alpha:P\oplus P'\xrightarrow{\sim} \Lambda^n\), either still choosing a homomorphism \(a:P\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Lambda^n\), and a retraction \(b:\Lambda^n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P\) (take \(P=\ker(b)\)). Or \(f'=\alpha(f\oplus 0)\alpha^{-1} =af b\). We pose \(\operatorname{tr}(f) = \operatorname{tr}(f')\). This element of \(\Lambda^\natural\) depends only on \(f\): if another choice \(c:P\leftrightarrows \Lambda^m:d\) is made, we have \(a=a(dc)(ba)\), from where
\[ \operatorname{tr}(a f b) = \operatorname{tr}(adcbafb) = \operatorname{tr}(cbafbad) = \operatorname{tr}(cfd) \text{.} \]

If \(f\) is an endomorphism of a \(\Lambda\)-finished projective module \(\mathbb{Z}/2\)-graded, of components \(f_i^j:P^j\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P^i\), are installed
\[ \operatorname{tr}(f) = \operatorname{tr}(f_0^0) - \operatorname{tr}(f_1^1) \text{.} \]

If there is a risk of ambiguity, we will write instead \(\operatorname{tr}(f;P)\). If the homomorphisms \(f:P\leftrightarrows Q:g\) are homogeneous, we immediately check by reduction to the free case that
\[ \tag{4.2.1} \operatorname{tr}(f g) = \operatorname{tr}\left((-1)^{\deg f \deg g} g f\right) \text{.} \]

(crack the filtration to return to the case of a direct sum).

A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-graded module will be considered \(\mathbb{Z}/2\)-graded by degree parity. In particular, if \(f\) is an endomorphism of a limited complex of \(\Lambda\)-finished projective modules, one poses
\[ \tag{4.3.1} \operatorname{tr}(f) = \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}(f^i) \text{.} \]
According to (4.2.1), if \(f\) is homotope at zero, \(\operatorname{tr}(f) = 0\):
\[ \tag{4.3.2} f = d H + h D \rightarrow \operatorname{tr}(f) = 0 \text{.} \]

Either \(\mathsf{K}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\) the category of objects the complexes bounded of \(\Lambda\)-projective modules of the finite type, and arrows the morphisms of complexes taken to homotopy. According to (4.2.2), if \(f\) is an endomorphism of \((K,F)\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{DF}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\), we have

Be \(\Lambda\) a ring, and \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^-(\Lambda)\). Be \(\Lambda\) a Noetherian ring on the left, and \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^-(\Lambda)\). To be \(K\) in \(\mathsf{DF}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\), it is necessary and sufficient that it is of finished tor-dimensional and that the \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(K)\) be of finite type.

Proposition. With previous notations, if \(u\) is an endomorphism of \(F\) and \(v\) an endomorphism of \(M\), we have
\[ \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda^H(u\otimes v) = \operatorname{tr}_A^H(u)\operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(v) \text{.} \]
Proposition.

It is assumed that \(P=A[H]\); the endomorphism \(u\) is then the multiplication to the right \(m_x\) by an element \(x=\sum a_h h\) of \(A[H]\). Isomorphism (5.7.1) transforms \(u\otimes v\) into \(\sum a_h m_h\otimes h^{-1} v\), trace \(a_e\cdot\operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(v)\).
Be \(G\) a group extension of \(\mathbb{Z}\) by a finite group \(H\)

\[ 1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1 \text{,} \]

and \(G^+\) the reverse image of \(\mathbb{N}\). Be \(\Lambda\) a ring, and \(P\) a \(\Lambda[G^+]\)-module, projective as \(\Lambda[H]\)-module. If \(g\in G^+\), we note \(Z_g\) the centralizer of \(g\) in \(H\); the multiplication by \(g\) is an endomorphism of \(P\), seen as \(\Lambda[Z_g]\)-module. \(P\) being \(\Lambda[Z_g]\)-projective, a trace \(\operatorname{tr}_\Lambda^{Z_g}(g)\) is defined. 5.6, we have
\[ \tag{5.9.1} \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(g) = |Z_g| \cdot \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda^{Z_g}(g) \text{.} \]

Suppose \(\Lambda\) is an algebra on a commutative ring \(A\). For \(P\) a \(A[G^+]\)-module, projective as \(A[H]\)-module and \(M\) a \(\Lambda[G^+]\)-module, projective as \(\Lambda\)-module, \(\Lambda[G^+]\)-module \(P\otimes_A M\) (diagonal action of \(G^+\)) is projective as \(\Lambda[H]\)-module and after 5.8, for everything \(g\in G^+\), we have
\[ \tag{5.9.2} \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda^{Z_g}(g,P\otimes_A M) = \operatorname{tr}_A^{Z_g}(g,P) \cdot \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(g,M) \text{.} \]

Be \(P\) a \(\Lambda[G^+]\)-projective module as \(\Lambda[H]\)-module, and \(P_H\) the variable corners of \(H\) in \(P\). It is a \(\Lambda\)-projective module. The action of \(g\in G^+\) passes to the quotient, and defines an endomorphism of \(P_H\) that depends only on the image of \(g\) in \(\mathbb{N}\). We note \(F\) the action of the elements \(g\in G^+\) image \(1\).

Proposition. \(\operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(F,P_H) = \sum_{g\mapsto 1}' \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda^{Z_g}(g,P)\), whereu \(\sum_{g\mapsto 1}'\) means an extended sum to the classes of \(H\)-conjugation of elements of \(G^+\) image \(1\) in \(\mathbb{N}\). Proposition.
After multiplication by \(|H|\), this formula can be verified as follows:

\[ |H|\cdot \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(F,P_H) = \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda\left(\sum_{g\mapsto 1} g,P\right) = \sum_{g\mapsto 1}' \frac{|H|}{|Z_g|}\operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(g,P) = \sum_{g\mapsto 1}' |H|\cdot \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda^{Z_g}(g,P) \text{.} \]

Choose an element \(g\in G^+\) image \(1\) in \(\mathbb{N}\), and either \(\sigma\) automorphism \(\Lambda[H]\) induced by automorphism \(\operatorname{ad} g\) of \(H\). It is up to the same person to give himself a \(\Lambda[G^+]\)-module, or a \(\Lambda[H]\)–module with endomorphism \(\sigma\)-linear \(\gamma\): act \(g^n h\) by \(\gamma^n h\). In this language, the formula 5.11 takes the form: for \(P\) a \(\Lambda[H]\)-projective module and for \(\gamma\) an endomorphism \(\sigma\)-linear of \(P\), we have
\[ \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(\gamma,P_H) = \sum_h' \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda^{Z_g}(h\gamma,P)\text{,} \]
where \(\sum'\) is a sum on the classes of \(\operatorname{ad}g\)-conjugation in \(H\) (orbits of \(H\) acting on itself by \(x\mapsto h x \operatorname{ad} g(h)^{-1}\)).

In this form, the properties of the traces immediately return us to the case where \(P=\Lambda[H]\). Endomorphism \(Y\) then has the form \(x\mapsto \sigma(x) a\), with \(a=\sum a_h h\) in \(\Lambda[H]\). Just treat the universal case where they \(a_h\) are undetermined. In this case, \(\Lambda=\mathbb{Z}\langle a_h\rangle_{h\in H}\) (non-communitative polynomials) and \(\Lambda^\natural\) is not twisted: we obtain 5.11 by the argument of the beginning and division by \(|H|\). It is also possible to calculate directly.
Everything that has been said above applies to modules \(\mathbb{Z}/2\)-graded, or to module complexes (cf. 4.2, 4.3).

Let's take over the ratings of 5.2, and either \(A=\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\), \(n\) being large enough for \(\Lambda\) to be a \(A\)-algebra. Either \(f:V_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U_0\) a finite and related coating of \(U_0\), Galoisian group of Galois \(H\), and such that the sheaf \(f^*\mathscr{G}_0\) is constant. We note \(M\) the constant value \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(V_0,f^*\mathscr{G}_0)\), it is a \(\Lambda[H]\)-module, projective as \(\Lambda\)-module.

The sheaf \(f_* A\) on \(U_0\), equipped with the natural action of \(H\), is a locally free sheaf (range one) of \(A[H]\)-modules. The complex \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,f_* A)\) is therefore the object of \(\mathsf{D}_\text{parf}\left(A[H]\right)\). It is equipped with an endomorphism of Frobenius \(F^*\).

For the natural action of \(H\) on \(f_* f^*\mathscr{G}_0\), trace morphism \(f_* f^*\mathscr{G}_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}_0\) is factored by isomorphism
\[ \left(f_* f^*\mathscr{G}_0\right)_H \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}_0 \text{.} \]
In addition, we have \(f_* f^*\mathscr{G}_0=f_* A\otimes_A M\) (with diagonal action of \(H\)). Isomorphism
\[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{G}_0 & (f_* A\otimes_A M)_H = (f_* A\otimes_A M)\otimes_{\Lambda[H]} \Lambda \ar[l]_-\sim} \]
is drifting away (4.12) and isomorphism
\[ \mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,\mathscr{G}) \sim \left(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,f_* A)\otimes_A M\right)\otimes_{\Lambda[H]} \Lambda \text{.} \]

The endomorphism \(F^*\) of the left member is deduced from the endomorphism \(F^*\) of \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,f_* A)\). The complex of \(\Lambda[H]\)-modules \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,f_* A)\), equipped with \(F^*\), can be seen as a complex of \(\Lambda[G^+]\)-modules, for \(G^+=H\times \mathbb{N}\). 5.10 and (5.9.2), amplified by 5.11.1, therefore provide
\[ \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,\mathscr{G})\right) = \sum_h' \operatorname{tr}_A^{Z_g}\left(h F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,f_* A)\right)\cdot \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(h,M) \]
where \(\sum'\) is a sum on conjugation classes in \(H\), In addition,
\[ |Z_h|\cdot \operatorname{tr}_A^{Z_h}\left(h F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,f_ A)\right) = \operatorname{tr}_A\left((F h^{-1})^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(V,A)\right) \text{.} \]
This formula remains true for \(A=\mathbb{Z}/\ell^m\), with \(m\) increasing. Moving to limit, we find that \(\operatorname{tr}_A^{Z_g}\left(h F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,f^* A)\right)\) is valid
\[ \frac{1}{|Z_h|} \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}\left((F h^{-1})^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(V,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\right) \]
(an element of \(\mathbb{Z}_\ell\)), and

\[ \tag{5.12.1} \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,\mathscr{G})\right) = \sum_h' \frac{1}{|Z_h|} \operatorname{tr}\left((F h^{-1})^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(V,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\right)\cdot \operatorname{tr}_\Lambda(h,M) \text{.} \]
It is now time to implement 5.4 (Note that if \(\overline{V}\) is the non-specific projective addition of \(V\), the fixed points of \(F h^{-1}\) are all multiplicity one).
1. If \(U^F=\varnothing\), \(\operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,\mathscr{G})\right) = 0\).

Indeed, \(F h^{-1}\) has no fixed point on \(V\), 5.4 shows that \(\sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}\left((F h^{-1})^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(V,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\right)=0\) and is applied (5.12.1).

1. In the general case, i.e. \(j:U'=U\setminus U^F\hookrightarrow U\). The filtration \(j_! j^* \mathscr{G}_0\subset \mathscr{G}_0\) of \(\mathscr{G}_0\) induces a filtration of \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,\mathscr{G})\) of successive quotients \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U',\mathscr{G})\) and \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U^F,\mathscr{G})\).4.4.1) and 5.1, we have

    \[ \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,\mathscr{G})\right) = \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U',\mathscr{G})\right) + \sum_{x\in U^F} \operatorname{tr}(F^*,\mathscr{G}_x) \text{,} \]

6 End of proof of 4.10

Prove 4.10, under the additional assumptions that the ring \(\Lambda\) is cancelled by a power of a prime number \(\ell\ne p\), and that \(n=1\). The general case will result: decompose \(\Lambda\) into its components \(\ell\)-primary (\(\ell\) traversing the prime numbers), and replace \(\mathbb{F}_q\) by \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\), \(X_0\) by \(X_0\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\).

For \(X_0\) separate finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(\mathscr{K}_0\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\text{ctf}(X_0,\Lambda)\), posions

\[ T'(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = \sum_{x\in X^F} \operatorname{tr}(F^*,\mathscr{K}_x) \]

and

\[ T''(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = \operatorname{tr}(F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K})) \text{.} \]

We have to prove the identity.

\[ (1;X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) \qquad T'(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = T''(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) \text{.} \]

First of all, we observe that \(T'\) and \(T''\) (generally noted) \(T\)) obey the same formalism:

(A) If \(j:U_0\hookrightarrow X_0\) is an open \(X_0\), additional closure \(Y_0\), we have

\[ T(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = T(U_0,\mathscr{K}_0|U_0) + T(Y_0,\mathscr{K}_0|Y_0) \text{.} \]

This is clear for \(T'\). For \(T''\), it is observed that the filtration \(0\subset j_! j^* \mathscr{K}_0\subset \mathscr{K}_0\) of \(\mathscr{K}_0\) induces a filtration of \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K})\), successive quotients \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(U,\mathscr{K})\) and \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(Y,\mathscr{K})\), and is invoked (4.4.1) (more precisely stated: \(\mathscr{K}_0\), equipped with the said filtrations, defines an object of \(\mathsf{DF}(X,\Lambda)\); by application of \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c\), an object of \(\mathsf{DF}_\text{parf}(\Lambda)\), to which \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K})\) is underlying, and graduated as said).

(B) If \(\mathscr{K}_0\) is a bounded complex with constructible and flat components,

\[ T(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = \sum (-1)^i T(X_0,\mathscr{K}^i) \text{.} \]

This is clear for \(T'\); for \(T''\), the above argument is applied to the stupid filtration of \(\mathscr{K}_0\), to obtain

\[ T''(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = \sum T''(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0^i[-i]) = \sum (-1)^i T''(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0^i) \text{.} \]

(C) For \(f:X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_0\) a morphism, we have

\[ T''(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = T''(Y_0,\mathsf{R} f_! \mathscr{K}_0) \]

and the same identity applies to \(T'\) if the fibres of \(f\) at the rational points of \(Y_0\) checking \(\left(1;f^{-1}(y),\mathscr{K}_0|f^{-1}(y)\right)\): we use that

\[ T'(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = \sum_{y\in Y^F} T'\left(f^{-1}(y), \mathscr{K}_0|f^{-1}(y)\right) \]

and

\[ \mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(Y, \mathsf{R} f_! \mathscr{K}) = \mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K}) \text{.} \]

Using this formalism, and 4.7, we bring back the proof of 4.10 to both individuals 5.1 and 5.2.
